items.prompt: True or false: Two figures are called similar if they have the same shape but not necessarily the same size.
items.quote: Two similar figures have the same shape but not necessarily the same size.

items.prompt: For two polygons of the same number of sides to be similar, the chapter requires (i) their corresponding angles are equal and (ii) their corresponding sides are in the same ratio. Are both conditions required?
items.quote: Two polygons of the same number of sides are similar, if (i) their corresponding angles are equal and (ii) their corresponding sides are in the same ratio (or proportion).

items.prompt: Is either one of the two conditions (equal corresponding angles, or proportional corresponding sides) sufficient on its own for two polygons to be similar?
items.answer: No, neither condition alone is sufficient

expected.correct: No, neither condition alone is sufficient
options: No, neither condition alone is sufficient
Yes, equal angles alone is sufficient
Yes, proportional sides alone is sufficient
Yes, either one alone is sufficient

items.quote: Thus, either of the above two conditions (i) and (ii) of similarity of two polygons is not sufficient for them to be similar.

items.prompt: In Activity 1 the shadow quadrilateral A′B′C′D′ is similar to ABCD with the correspondence A′↔A, B′↔B, C′↔C, D′↔D. Which vertex of A′B′C′D′ corresponds to vertex C of ABCD?
items.quote: vertex A′ corresponds to vertex A, vertex B′ corresponds to vertex B, vertex C′ corresponds to vertex C and vertex D′ corresponds to vertex D

items.prompt: For triangles ABC and DEF with A↔D, B↔E, C↔F, which similarity statement uses correct correspondence: Δ ABC ~ Δ EDF, Δ ABC ~ Δ FED, or Δ BAC ~ Δ EDF?
items.quote: we cannot write Δ ABC ~ Δ EDF or Δ ABC ~ Δ FED. However, we can write Δ BAC ~ Δ EDF.

items.prompt: In Δ ABC a line DE ‖ BC meets AB at D and AC at E. By the Basic Proportionality Theorem, AD/DB equals which ratio?
items.quote: So, AD/DB = AE/EC     (Theorem 6.1)

items.prompt: In the proof of BPT, why is ar(BDE) = ar(DEC)? Because the two triangles are on the same base DE and between the same parallels — true or false?
items.quote: Note that Δ BDE and DEC are on the same base DE and between the same parallels BC and DE.

items.prompt: In Δ PQR, S on PQ and T on PR satisfy PS/SQ = PT/TR. By the converse of the Basic Proportionality Theorem, what can you conclude about ST?
items.quote: So, ST ‖ QR     (Theorem 6.2)

items.prompt: In Δ PQR, PE = 3.9 cm, EQ = 3 cm, PF = 3.6 cm, FR = 2.4 cm. Compute PE/EQ as a decimal.
items.quote: (i) PE = 3.9 cm, EQ = 3 cm, PF = 3.6 cm and FR = 2.4 cm

items.prompt: Starting from AD/DB = AE/EC, invert each side and add 1 to both sides. What does AB/AD equal?
items.quote: or, AB/AD = AC/AE

items.prompt: If two angles of one triangle are respectively equal to two angles of another triangle, are the triangles similar?
items.answer: Yes, by the AA similarity criterion

expected.correct: Yes, by the AA similarity criterion
options: Yes, by the AA similarity criterion
No, you need all three angles
No, you also need a side ratio
Only if they are right triangles

items.quote: If two angles of one triangle are respectively equal to two angles of another triangle, then the two triangles are similar.

items.prompt: In Example 4, PQ ‖ RS. The reason given for ∠P = ∠S is which angle relationship?
items.quote: So, ∠ P = ∠ S     (Alternate angles)

items.prompt: Two triangles have all three pairs of corresponding sides in the same ratio. Which similarity criterion proves them similar?
items.quote: So, Δ ABC ~ Δ RQP     (SSS similarity)

items.prompt: Simplify the ratio 3.8/7.6 to lowest terms (as a decimal).
Given OA/OC = OD/OB and ∠AOD = ∠COB (vertically opposite angles, included between the proportional sides), which criterion proves Δ AOD ~ Δ COB?
items.quote: Therefore, from (1) and (2), Δ AOD ~ Δ COB     (SAS similarity criterion)

items.prompt: Given OA · OB = OC · OD, rearrange into a proportion of the sides including the vertical angle. OA/OC equals what?
items.quote: So, OA/OC = OD/OB     (1)

items.prompt: If Δ ABC ~ Δ PQR, the side ratio AB/PQ equals which other ratios?
items.quote: So, AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP     (1)

items.prompt: After Δ ABC ~ Δ RQP, ∠C = ∠P. Using the angle-sum property with ∠A = 80° and ∠B = 60°, find ∠P (in degrees).
items.quote: But ∠ C = 180° – ∠ A – ∠ B     (Angle sum property)

items.prompt: Solving (4.8 + x)/x = 3.6/0.9 gives 4.8 + x = 4x. Find x (in metres), the length of the girl's shadow.
A vertical pole 6 m casts a 4 m shadow while a tower casts a 28 m shadow at the same time. Using similar triangles (height/shadow constant), find the tower's height in metres.
items.quote: 15. A vertical pole of length 6 m casts a shadow 4 m long on the ground and at the same time a tower casts a shadow 28 m long. Find the height of the tower.

items.prompt: In Example 8, CM and RN are medians, so AB = 2 AM and PQ = 2 PN. After substituting into AB/PQ = CA/RP and cancelling the factor 2, AM/PN equals which ratio?
items.quote: i.e., AM/PN = CA/RP     (3)

items.prompt: In Example 6, line segments AB and CD intersect at O with OA·OB = OC·OD, which rearranges to OA/OC = OD/OB, and ∠AOD = ∠COB are vertically opposite angles included between those proportional sides. Which similarity criterion establishes Δ AOD ~ Δ COB?
items.quote: Therefore, from (1) and (2), Δ AOD ~ Δ COB     (SAS similarity criterion)
